title: Immediate Action: Cat Lying on Side and Paddling Legs | Vet Triage
meta_desc: If your cat is lying on their side and paddling their legs involuntarily, they may be having a seizure. This is a high-priority medical emergency.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat is lying on its side and paddling its legs, it is likely experiencing a generalized seizure, which is a critical medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention.

What does it mean if my cat is lying on its side and performing paddling motions?

When a cat lies on their side and performs involuntary paddling motions with their limbs, it is typically a sign of a generalized seizure. This occurs due to a sudden surge of electrical activity in the brain. During such an episode, the cat is usually unconscious and unaware of their surroundings. They may also exhibit jaw chomping, excessive salivation, or involuntary urination and defecation.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is paddling while lying down?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical medical emergency.
  • Even if the episode lasts only a minute or two, it can be a sign of severe underlying issues such as toxins, metabolic disease, or neurological disorders.
  • If the paddling lasts for more than five minutes (status epilepticus), it can lead to permanent brain damage or life-threatening hyperthermia.
  • You should contact the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How can a video of my cat's behavior help a veterinarian with triage?

  • If it is safe to do so, capture a short video of the paddling behavior to show your veterinarian.
  • Many different conditions, including fainting (syncope) or vestibular episodes, can look similar to seizures.
  • A video allows the vet to analyze specific motor patterns and the cat's level of consciousness.
  • This evidence helps differentiate between a neurological event and a cardiovascular one, leading to a faster diagnosis and treatment plan.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of cardiopulmonary arrest (CPA) in an unconscious cat, lack of response to stimuli or limp body tone are unreliable indicators. Owners should watch for chest excursions and check for corneal or palpebral reflexes; absence of either suggests CPA. Compromised breathing manifests as increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by altered respiratory patterns; cats may sit crouched with the sternum slightly elevated. Labored, open-mouth breathing and cyanosis indicate significant pulmonary dysfunction and impending pulmonary arrest. In cases of suspected arterial thromboembolism, misdiagnosis of neurologic abnormalities is common; while the clot may lyse spontaneously within 1-72 hours, residual problems can occur.

Chapter: Emergency, General Principles, Cardiopulmonary

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1659)

faqs.question: Should I try to restrain my cat while they are paddling?
faqs.answer: No. Never put your hands near your cat's mouth or try to hold them down, as they may bite or scratch involuntarily. Instead, clear away any sharp objects or furniture that might cause injury.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately after the episode ends?
faqs.answer: Keep the lights low and the environment quiet. Cats are often disoriented, blind, or anxious immediately after a seizure (the post-ictal phase). Transport them to a vet immediately even if they seem to be recovering.

faqs.question: Could this be something other than a seizure?
faqs.answer: Yes, while paddling is a classic seizure sign, it can also be associated with severe heart issues or sudden vestibular (balance) failure. This is why immediate professional evaluation is required.
